pages.title: Linear Algebra
pages.text: Lecture 01ÔľöThe Geometry of Linear Equations Row Picture Column Picture Matrix Picture Matrix Multiplication Linear Independence Lecture 01ÔľöThe Geometry of Linear Equations The fundamental problem of linear algebra is to solve n linear equations in n unknowns, for example: 2x‚ąíy = 0‚ąíx+2y = 3\begin{aligned} 2x-y\,=\,0\\ -x+2y\,=\,3 \end{aligned} 2x‚ąíy=0‚ąíx+2y=3‚Äč In the first lecture, Dr. Strang show us three ways to view this problem. Row Picture Plot the points that satisfied each equation. The intersection of the plots (if they do intersect) is the solution of the system of above equations, which is x = 1,y = 2x\,=\,1, y\,=\,2x=1,y=2. Figure 1: The plots of the above equations intersect at the point (1,2) We substitute (1,2) into the original system of equations to check it‚Äôs validity: 2‚čÖ1‚ąí2 = 0‚ąí1+2‚čÖ2 = 3\begin{aligned} 2\cdot1-2\,=\,0\\ -1+2\cdot2\,=\,3 \end{aligned} 2‚čÖ1‚ąí2=0‚ąí1+2‚čÖ2=3‚Äč Similarly, the solution of a three dimensional system is the common intersection of those three planes (if there does exist one). Column Picture In the column picture, we rewrite the system as a single equation by turning the coefficients in the column of the system into vectors: x[21]+y[‚ąí12]=[03]\begin{aligned} x \left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\\ 1 \end{array} \right] +y \left[ \begin{array}{c} -1\\ 2 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0\\ 3 \end{array} \right] \end{aligned} x[21‚Äč]+y[‚ąí12‚Äč]=[03‚Äč]‚Äč Given two vectors c and d and scalars x and y, the sum xc+yd is called a linear combination of c and d, which is an important concept throughout Linear Algebra. Geometrically, we are looking for a pair of x and y which satisfies that x copies of vector [2‚ąí1]\left[\begin{array}{c}2\\-1\end{array}\right][2‚ąí1‚Äč] added to y copies of vector [‚ąí12]\left[\begin{array}{c}-1\\2\end{array}\right][‚ąí12‚Äč] equals the vector [03]\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\3\end{array}\right][03‚Äč]. As shown in Figure 2, x=1 and y=2x=1 \;\text{and}\; y=2x=1andy=2 agreeing with the result we got from row picture. Figure 2: A linear combination of the column vectors. In the three dimensions, the column picture requires to find a linear combination of 3-dimensional vectors that equals to the vector b. Matrix Picture Rewrite the system of equations as a single equation by using matrices and vectors: [2‚ąí1‚ąí12][xy]=[03]\begin{aligned} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1\\ -1 & 2\\ \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} x\\ y \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 0\\ 3 \end{array} \right] \end{aligned} [2‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąí12‚Äč][xy‚Äč]=[03‚Äč]‚Äč The matrix [2‚ąí1‚ąí12]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2&-1\\-1&2\end{array}\right][2‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąí12‚Äč] is called the coefficient matrix. The vector x=[xy]\bold{x}=\left[\begin{array}{c}x\\y\end{array}\right]x=[xy‚Äč] is the vector of unknowns. The value on the right hand side of the equations form the vector b: Ax=bA\bold{x}=\bold{b} Ax=b The three dimensional matrix picture is similar to the two dimensional one, except that the vectors and matrices increase in size. Matrix Multiplication How do we multiply a matrix A by a vector x? [2513][12]= ?\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\ 1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =\;? [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=? The method Dr. Strang suggests is to think of the entries of x as the coefficients of a linear combination of the column vectors of the matrix: [2513][12]=1[21]+2[53]=[127]\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =1\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\\1 \end{array} \right] +2\left[ \begin{array}{c} 5\\3 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 12\\7 \end{array} \right] [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=1[21‚Äč]+2[53‚Äč]=[127‚Äč] The technique shows that Ax is a linear combination of the columns of A. Also, you can calculate the product Ax by taking dot product of each row of A with the vector x: [2513][12]=[2‚čÖ1+5‚čÖ21‚čÖ1+3‚čÖ2]=[127]\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\cdot1+5\cdot2\\1\cdot1+3\cdot2 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 12\\7 \end{array} \right] [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=[2‚čÖ1+5‚čÖ21‚čÖ1+3‚čÖ2‚Äč]=[127‚Äč] Linear Independence In the column and matrix pictures, the right hand side of the equation is a vector b. Given a matrix A, if we can solve: Ax=bA\bold{x}=\bold{b} Ax=b for every possible b, we say that A is an inversible matrix, which means that the linear combinations of the column vectors fill the xy-plane (in two dimensional case). Otherwise, we say that A is a singular matrix, whose column vectors are linear dependent, or in other words, all linear combinations of those vectors lie on a point or line (in two dimensional case). In such case, the combinations don‚Äôt fill the whole space.

Furthermore, if xB‚Č•0 old{x_B}\geq0xB‚Äč‚Č•0, then x is a basic feasible solution‚Äĺ\underline{basic\ feasible\ solution}basic feasible solution‚Äč. We can see, when A does not have full rank, then either Ax = b has no solution‚Äĺ\underline{no\ solution}no solution‚Äč and hence P=0P=0P=0, or some constraints are redundant (which can be avoided by preprocessing matrix A). Thus, A point x in P is an extreme point of P if and only if x is a basic feasible solution corresponding to some basis B. The polyhedron P has only a finite number of extreme points (less than CmnC_m^nCmn‚Äč). What do extreme points bring us? When P={x‚ąąRn‚ą£Ax=b,x‚Č•0}P=\{ old{x\in R^n|Ax=b,x\geq0}\}P={x‚ąąRn‚ą£Ax=b,x‚Č•0} is a nonempty polytope, then any point in P can be represented as a convex combination of the extreme points of P. If P is unbounded, there exists an extremal direction. Definition: A vector d(‚Č†0)‚ąąRn old{d(
eq0)\in R^n}d(ÓÄ†‚Äč=0)‚ąąRn is an extremal direction of P, if {x‚ąąRn‚ą£x=x0+őĽd, őĽ‚Č•0}‚äāP\{ old{x\in R^n|x=x_0+\lambda d,\ \lambda\geq0\}\subset }P{x‚ąąRn‚ą£x=x0‚Äč+őĽd, őĽ‚Č•0}‚äāP for all x0‚ąąP old{x_0}\in Px0‚Äč‚ąąP. d satisfies Ad=0 old{Ad=0}Ad=0 and d‚Č•0 old{d\geq 0}d‚Č•0. Resolution theorem Let V={vi‚ąąRn‚ą£i‚ąąI} old{V = \{v_i\in R^n|}i\in I\}V={vi‚Äč‚ąąRn‚ą£i‚ąąI} be a set of extreme points of P, then for ‚ąÄx‚ąąP orall x \in P‚ąÄx‚ąąP x=‚ąĎi‚ąąIőĽivi+d old{x}=\sum\limits_{i\in I} \lambda_iv_i+ old{d} x=i‚ąąI‚ąĎ‚ÄčőĽi‚Äčvi‚Äč+d where ‚ąĎi‚ąąIőĽi=1, őĽi‚Č•0, ‚ąÄi‚ąąI.\sum\limits_{i\in I}\lambda_i=1,\ \lambda_i\geq0,\  orall i\in I. i‚ąąI‚ąĎ‚ÄčőĽi‚Äč=1, őĽi‚Äč‚Č•0, ‚ąÄi‚ąąI. and either d=0 old{d=0}d=0 or d is an extremal direction of P Fundamental theorem of LP For a standard form LP, if its feasible domain P is nonempty, then the optimal objective value of z=cTx over P old{z=c^Tx}\ over\ Pz=cTx over P is either unbounded below, or it is attained at (at least) an extreme point of P. Lecture 4: Simplex Method Basic Idea: Phase 1: Step 1 (Starting): Find an initial extreme point (ep) or declare P in null. Phase 2: Step 2 (Checking optimality): if the current eo is optimal, STOP. Step 3 (Pivoting): Move to a better ep, then return to Step 2. If we do not repeat using the same extreme point, then the algorithm will always terminates in finite number of iterations. But how to efficiently generate better extreme points? In the algebra term, we just need to use basic feasible solution to replace extreme point above. If there exists at least one basic variable becoming zero, the extreme point may correspond to more than one basic feasible solution, in this case we say the bfs is a degenerate one. Nondegeneracy Property 1: If a bfs x is nondegenerate, then x is uniquely determined by n hyperplanes. Let M=[BN0I] old{M=}\left[ egin{array}{cc}  old{B} &  old{N}\  old0 &  old{I} \end{array}ight]M=[B0‚ÄčNI‚Äč], M is a nonsingular matrix. x is uniquely determined by n linearly independent hyperplanes since Mx=[BN0I][xBxN]=[b0] old{Mx}=\left[ egin{array}{cc}  old{B} &  old{N}\  old{0} &  old{I} \end{array}ight]\left[ egin{array}{c}  old{x_B}\  old{x_N} \end{array}ight]=\left[ egin{array}{c}  old{b}\  old{0} \end{array}ight] Mx=[B0‚ÄčNI‚Äč][xB‚ÄčxN‚Äč‚Äč]=[b0‚Äč] x=[xBxN]=M‚ąí1[b0] old{x}=\left[ egin{array}{c}  old{x_B}\ old{x_N} \end{array}ight]= old{M^{-1}}\left[ egin{array}{c}  old{b}\  old{0} \end{array}ight] x=[xB‚ÄčxN‚Äč‚Äč]=M‚ąí1[b0‚Äč] in which, M‚ąí1=[B‚ąí1‚ąíB‚ąí1N0I] old{M^{-1}}=\left[ egin{array}{cc}  old{B^{-1}} &  old{-B^{-1}N}\  old{0} &  old{I} \end{array}ight] M‚ąí1=[B‚ąí10‚Äč‚ąíB‚ąí1NI‚Äč] We call M‚ąí1 old{M^{-1}}M‚ąí1 (or M) the fundamental matrix of LP. Property 2: If a bfs x is degenerate, then x is overdetermined by more than n hyperplanes. Other than n hyperplanes determined by non-basic variables, there exists at least one basic variable that xi=0x_i=0xi‚Äč=0, which indicates an extra hyperplane. Simplex method under nondegeneracy Basic idea: Instead of considering all bfs at the same time, wo just consider some neighboring bfs, moving from one bfs to another with a simple pivoting scheme. Definition Two basic feasible solution are adjacent if they have m-1	extit{m-1}m-1 basic variables in common. Each bfs has m-n	extit{m-n}m-n adjacent neighbors, which can be reached by increasing one non basic variable from zero to positive and decrease one basic variable to 0, called pivoting.

pages.title: LA01: The geometry of linear equations
pages.text: The fundamental problem of linear algebra is to solve n linear equations in n unknowns, for example: 2x‚ąíy = 0‚ąíx+2y = 3\begin{aligned} 2x-y\,=\,0\\ -x+2y\,=\,3 \end{aligned} 2x‚ąíy=0‚ąíx+2y=3‚Äč In the first lecture, Dr. Strang show us three ways to view this problem. Row Picture Plot the points that satisfied each equation. The intersection of the plots (if they do intersect) is the solution of the system of above equations, which is x = 1,y = 2x\,=\,1, y\,=\,2x=1,y=2. Figure 1: The plots of the above equations intersect at the point (1,2) We substitute (1,2) into the original system of equations to check it‚Äôs validity: 2‚čÖ1‚ąí2 = 0‚ąí1+2‚čÖ2 = 3\begin{aligned} 2\cdot1-2\,=\,0\\ -1+2\cdot2\,=\,3 \end{aligned} 2‚čÖ1‚ąí2=0‚ąí1+2‚čÖ2=3‚Äč Similarly, the solution of a three dimensional system is the common intersection of those three planes (if there does exist one). Column Picture In the column picture, we rewrite the system as a single equation by turning the coefficients in the column of the system into vectors: x[21]+y[‚ąí12]=[03]\begin{aligned} x \left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\\ 1 \end{array} \right] +y \left[ \begin{array}{c} -1\\ 2 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} 0\\ 3 \end{array} \right] \end{aligned} x[21‚Äč]+y[‚ąí12‚Äč]=[03‚Äč]‚Äč Given two vectors c and d and scalars x and y, the sum xc+yd is called a linear combination of c and d, which is an important concept throughout Linear Algebra. Geometrically, we are looking for a pair of x and y which satisfies that x copies of vector [2‚ąí1]\left[\begin{array}{c}2\\-1\end{array}\right][2‚ąí1‚Äč] added to y copies of vector [‚ąí12]\left[\begin{array}{c}-1\\2\end{array}\right][‚ąí12‚Äč] equals the vector [03]\left[\begin{array}{c}0\\3\end{array}\right][03‚Äč]. As shown in Figure 2, x=1 and y=2x=1 \;\text{and}\; y=2x=1andy=2 agreeing with the result we got from row picture. Figure 2: A linear combination of the column vectors. In the three dimensions, the column picture requires to find a linear combination of 3-dimensional vectors that equals to the vector b. Matrix Picture Rewrite the system of equations as a single equation by using matrices and vectors: [2‚ąí1‚ąí12][xy]=[03]\begin{aligned} \left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1\\ -1 & 2\\ \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} x\\ y \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 0\\ 3 \end{array} \right] \end{aligned} [2‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąí12‚Äč][xy‚Äč]=[03‚Äč]‚Äč The matrix [2‚ąí1‚ąí12]\left[\begin{array}{cc}2&-1\\-1&2\end{array}\right][2‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąí12‚Äč] is called the coefficient matrix. The vector x=[xy]\bold{x}=\left[\begin{array}{c}x\\y\end{array}\right]x=[xy‚Äč] is the vector of unknowns. The value on the right hand side of the equations form the vector b: Ax=bA\bold{x}=\bold{b} Ax=b The three dimensional matrix picture is similar to the two dimensional one, except that the vectors and matrices increase in size. Matrix Multiplication How do we multiply a matrix A by a vector x? [2513][12]= ?\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\ 1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =\;? [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=? The method Dr. Strang suggests is to think of the entries of x as the coefficients of a linear combination of the column vectors of the matrix: [2513][12]=1[21]+2[53]=[127]\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =1\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\\1 \end{array} \right] +2\left[ \begin{array}{c} 5\\3 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 12\\7 \end{array} \right] [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=1[21‚Äč]+2[53‚Äč]=[127‚Äč] The technique shows that Ax is a linear combination of the columns of A. Also, you can calculate the product Ax by taking dot product of each row of A with the vector x: [2513][12]=[2‚čÖ1+5‚čÖ21‚čÖ1+3‚čÖ2]=[127]\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2&5\\1&3 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} 1\\2 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2\cdot1+5\cdot2\\1\cdot1+3\cdot2 \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array}{c} 12\\7 \end{array} \right] [21‚Äč53‚Äč][12‚Äč]=[2‚čÖ1+5‚čÖ21‚čÖ1+3‚čÖ2‚Äč]=[127‚Äč] Linear Independence In the column and matrix pictures, the right hand side of the equation is a vector b. Given a matrix A, if we can solve: Ax=bA\bold{x}=\bold{b} Ax=b for every possible b, we say that A is an inversible matrix, which means that the linear combinations of the column vectors fill the xy-plane (in two dimensional case). Otherwise, we say that A is a singular matrix, whose column vectors are linear dependent, or in other words, all linear combinations of those vectors lie on a point or line (in two dimensional case). In such case, the combinations don‚Äôt fill the whole space.
